exemplar.title: How Does the Surface Area of a Liquid Affect its Rate of Evaporation?

detailedExemplarData.title: How Does the Surface Area of a Liquid Affect its Rate of Evaporation?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables are well formatted with clear headings, units, and appropriate significant figures.
Uncertainty propagation in surface area calculations is correctly applied in at least one worked example.
Measurements of radii and volumes are tabulated clearly, enabling accurate geometry calculations.
weaknesses: Tables of processed volume changes lack associated uncertainties, undermining confidence in rate calculations.
Calculation of evaporation rates omits the surface-area term and misapplies the formula in worked examples.
Graphs lack error bars and trend-line fitting, limiting insight into data relationships.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data is clear and precise in places, warranting mid-band credit. Uncertainties are considered but not propagated consistently. Significant processing errors (rate formula misapplication) and omitted uncertainty propagation in key tables reduce reliability. A score of 3 balances correct elements against substantive inaccuracies and omissions.

strengths: The research question is set in a vivid, specific context grounded in a real-world observation, enhancing relevance.
The controlled-variables table and inclusion of photographs support clarity and reproducibility.
Apparatus lists and a nine-step method specify volumes, timing intervals, and repeat counts, allowing replication with few ambiguities.
weaknesses: Method omits details on beaker positioning (to prevent draughts) and trial randomization, risking systematic bias.
Background discussion lacks quantitative linkage to theory (e.g. latent heat, vapour pressure equations).
Environmental factors such as humidity or minor drafts are not addressed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides a well-framed research question tied to everyday observation, a detailed reproducible method, and relevant methodological considerations. However, minor omissions (trial order, environmental controls) and lack of quantitative theory prevent top-band performance. A holistic score of 4 reflects strong contextualization and reproducibility, with room for deeper theoretical justification.

strengths: The conclusion reiterates the hypothesis and makes referenceâeven if minimalâto observed data trends.
weaknesses: The conclusion is not supported by quantitative analysis, relying largely on qualitative impressions.
Comparison to accepted scientific context is superficial, lacking quantitative or literature-based justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student states a conclusion in line with their hypothesis but fails to justify it through the presented analysis, fitting the 1â2 descriptor. Slight reference to data trends justifies awarding 2 rather than 1.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are described with attention to their relative severity.
Improvements are realistic, relevant, and accompanied by explanations regarding practicality and expected effect.
The evaluation integrates thoughtful reflection on limitations and links each to a proposed corrective measure.
weaknesses: Quantitative estimates of how each improvement would reduce uncertainty are not provided.
The discussion could better link each limitation to its numerical impact on results.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation clearly identifies and explains multiple methodological limitations and proposes well-justified improvements. Although deeper quantitative linkage would achieve perfection, the depth of reflection and relevance of suggestions warrant a high score of 5.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated and immediately framed through the studentâs bedtime observation of hot water cooling; this everyday anecdote, together with the succinct discussion of evaporation physics, provides a **specific, relevant context** that motivates the investigation.  Because the context is clearly tied to the variable under study (surface area) rather than a generic description of evaporation, it fits the descriptor of a specific and appropriate context.  Minor stylistic issues do not detract from clarity, so the strand is best placed in the 5â6 band and awarded 5 marks.

Uncertainties are given for radius, volume, time and propagated for one worked example; percentage uncertainties are quoted and later discussed.  Nevertheless, many uncertainties (e.g. for all volume readings and for temperature) are not propagated through every calculation, and the final graph lacks error bars.  These **significant omissions** place the work in the 3â4 band but nearer its lower end, so 3 marks are appropriate.

The student identifies and explains control of volume, initial temperature, room temperature and timing, showing awareness of **relevant methodological considerations** for collecting sufficient data.  However, the discussion stops at description; there is no deeper explanation of why six trials or why 10-minute intervals optimise data quality, nor is there justification of using volume as a mass proxy.  Because the considerations are described rather than fully **explained**, the work fits solidly in the middle of the 3â4 band, so 3 marks are appropriate.

Three distinct limitations (time constraint, unequal surface areas, transfer losses) are **described in detail**, and their qualitative impact (moderate, very high, insignificant) is articulated, demonstrating an understanding of relative severity.  The analysis, however, stops short of a fully reasoned explanation of how each weakness propagates into numerical uncertainty, so the strand aligns with the upper end of the 3â4 band (4 marks) rather than 5â6.

The apparatus list, photographs and nine-step method give enough detail (volumes, temperatures, timing intervals, six repeats) that another student could replicate the basic procedure with only **few ambiguities**âe.g. how the beakers were positioned to avoid draughts, or how repeat trials were organised.  Those small omissions prevent a fully unambiguous replication, but overall the description is sufficiently complete to merit the 3â4 band; the clearer end of that band (4 marks) is justified because volumes, instruments and timing are all specified quantitatively.

Data were processed to obtain surface areas, volume changes and an evaporationârate table, yet the student later admits calculation errors (e.g. rate formula misapplied by omitting area in sample calculation) and the graph produced from those rates shows no correlation, revealing **inaccuracies that undermine validity**.  Because there are multiple substantive errors but processing is not completely absent, the work falls in the 1â2 band; 2 marks are awarded rather than 1 because some correct conversions and propagated uncertainties are present.

For each weakness, the student proposes **realistic, relevant improvements** (using a fan, substituting uniform beakers, covering the cylinder) and discusses potential flaws or practicality, amounting to an explanation rather than a mere list.  Some rationale lacks quantitative depth, preventing a perfect score, but the explanations are credible and linked directly to identified errors, justifying placement in the 5â6 band with 5 marks.

Raw and processed data tables include titles, units, significant figures and clearly distinguished variables; graphs are present, though one axis label is minimal (âtimeâ).  Minor formatting clutter aside, the **communication is clear and mainly precise**, satisfying both criteria more than partially, therefore the top of the 3â4 band (4 marks) is warranted.

Beyond a brief statement that wider openings âspeed up evaporationâ in line with theory, there is little **comparison to accepted scientific context**; no quantitative comparison to literature values or referenced models is offered.  The discussion remains superficial, so the strand sits at the bottom of the 1â2 band (1 mark).

The student reiterates the hypothesis and offers a verdict, but openly concedes that the processed data do not support the claim, relying instead on qualitative observations.  Hence the conclusion is **stated but not supported by the analysis**, matching the 1â2 descriptor. 2 marks (upper end of the band) are given because some reference to data trends from Table 2 is attempted.

annotations.comment: The conclusion reiterates the hypothesis but is not justified by the presented data, relying instead on qualitative impressions.
Consider quantifying how a constant-speed fan would reduce measurement time or uncertainty, linking improvement to expected numerical gains.
The reflection notes the importance of full surface area calculation but could expand on the mathematical approach you will adopt in future.
Discuss how using lids on the graduated cylinder could quantitatively reduce transfer-loss errors by adding an uncertainty estimate for this step.
Ensure unit consistency by converting all volumes to liters or milliliters uniformly and adjusting significant figures accordingly.
The controlled-variables table on page 4 systematically links each variable to a clear control method, supporting reproducibility.
Including photographs of each beaker and apparatus enhances clarity and supports reproducibility by visually confirming dimensions and setup.
The method describes nine steps but does not specify trial order randomization or rest periods, which could introduce systematic bias across repeats.
Table 1 is well formatted with clear headings, units, and significant figures, and distinguishes variables consistently.
The introduction vividly ties the evening routine anecdote to the research question, providing a specific context that grounds the investigation in a real-world observation.
Consider expanding the context with quantitative theory, such as latent heat of vaporization or heat transfer equations, to deepen the scientific framing of your research question.
The physics background effectively describes evaporation qualitatively but lacks quantitative linkage to parameters like latent heat or vapour pressure equations.
The variables table clearly states IV, DV, and controls but omits environmental factors like air humidity or minor drafts that could influence evaporation.
Propagation of uncertainty in Table 4 correctly applies $$\frac{\Delta A}{A}=0.5\frac{\Delta r}{r}$$ and quotes absolute uncertainties for each surface area.
Table 5 calculates change in volume but lacks associated uncertainties, which undermines confidence in the rate calculations.
Table 2 omits error bars or uncertainties for each processed volume, reducing precision in later analysis and graph interpretation.
Measurements of beaker radii and volumes are tabulated clearly, enabling accurate calculation of surface areas.
Table 6âs rate formula appears to omit the surface area term in the denominator, conflicting with your stated method and leading to inconsistent results.
The example calculation of evaporation rate on page 9 misapplies $$\mathrm{rate}=\frac{\Delta m}{t\times A}$$, contributing to the graphâs lack of correlation.
When summing relative uncertainties, justify why each source is included and confirm if any contributions are negligible before aggregation.
The Google Sheets graph acknowledges no trend but lacks error bars or trend-line fitting to explore any subtle relationships in the data.
The narrative in the opening paragraph on page 11 relies on raw Table 2 rather than processed rates, weakening the link to your quantitative analysis.
The evaluation identifies specific errors and proposes realistic improvements, demonstrating thoughtful reflection on methodological limitations.
Link each limitation to its numerical impact on uncertainty (e.g., percentage change), which would strengthen your evaluation.
Detail calibration and precision specifications of the thermometer and balance in the apparatus section to clarify measurement uncertainties.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
